name: Strait of Hormuz
summary: Day 43. CEASEFIRE FAILED OPERATIONAL TEST. Three Chinese VLCCs halted at strait — NOT through. IRGC toll system intact. ~9 vessels through April 8-10 combined vs 100+/day pre-war = ~3% capacity. Stranded vessels revised sharply UP: 800-3,000 (Bloomberg/IMO/Global Security). 20,000+ seafarers trapped. Russia-China vetoed UNSC Hormuz resolution — no multilateral reopening pathway. Only bilateral (Islamabad) or unilateral (IRGC toll). War risk insurance 1.5-3.0% hull, 7-10x pre-war. LMA: safety, not insurance, is the bottleneck. CBS: oil exports return to previous levels by July 2026 at earliest. Iran demonstrated Hormuz as coercive toggle linked to Israeli compliance.

signals.content: IRGC announced Hormuz closed to US, Israeli, and Western allied ships. Shipping traffic reduced to a trickle — 21 tankers total since Feb 28 vs 100+/day pre-war (CNBC tracker data).
Dallas Federal Reserve described closure as 'largest disruption to energy supply since the 1970s energy crisis' and 'largest in the history of the global oil market.'
Mojtaba Khamenei's first statement confirmed Hormuz would remain closed to pressure Iran's enemies.
Insurance companies cancelling war risk coverage for vessels in Gulf. 150 ships stranded around the strait. Marine war risk insurance prices rising 50-100%.
Trump issued 48-hour ultimatum for traffic to resume, threatened to strike Iran's power plants. Hormuz closure entering 4th week with no resolution.

IRGC Navy chief Tangsiri killed at Bandar Abbas — the operational HQ for Hormuz closure. Impact on operations unclear: could disrupt IRGC naval coordination or harden resolve. (Sources: IDF, CENTCOM, Al Jazeera)

IRGC formalized tolled passage through Iranian territorial waters around Larak Island. Approved vessels receive code and IRGC escort. Yuan-denominated payment at ~$2M/transit. 142 transits March 1-25 vs 2,652 baseline = 95% reduction. Transforms blockade from military closure to controlled monetized chokepoint. (Sources: USNI News, Fortune, Lloyd's List Intelligence, NBC News)

No change in Hormuz transit data from March 27 snapshot. IRGC toll booth system continues operating — yuan-denominated payments, escorted passage around Larak Island. 142 transits March 1-25 vs 2,652 baseline (95% reduction). CENTCOM claims 92% of IRGC Navy's largest vessels destroyed but toll operations unaffected. Tangsiri assassination (March 26) has not visibly disrupted IRGC naval enforcement.

Pentagon preparing 'weeks of limited ground operations' including Kharg Island raids (Al Jazeera). Trump to FT: 'Maybe we take Kharg Island, maybe we don't.' 82nd Airborne + 2 MEUs deploying alongside USS Tripoli Marines. Brent pulled back to $107.71 (-4.3%) on diplomacy hopes — but ground ops prep suggests Hormuz military escalation simultaneously. Toll system continues operating. (Sources: Al Jazeera, Axios, CNN, FT)

EVENING UPDATE: Trump claimed (Times of Israel) that Iran agreed to let 'another 20 boats' through Hormuz as 'sign of respect,' beginning Monday. UNVERIFIED — Iran has NOT confirmed. Unclear whether incremental to existing toll system (~142 transits Mar 1-25) or Trump's recharacterization of existing traffic. Trump also told FT: 'maybe we take Kharg Island' — reiterating oil seizure rhetoric. IEA: 'closure of the Strait of Hormuz is the biggest oil shock in history.' Toll system continues. (Sources: Times of Israel, FT, IEA via CNBC)

Iran's selective Hormuz opening EXPANDING: FM Araghchi announced ships from China, Russia, India, Iraq, Pakistan allowed (March 26). Malaysia and Thailand subsequently added. Pakistan secured specific deal for 20 Pakistani-flagged vessels (2/day) — announced by Pakistan FM Dar. Strategy: split the coalition by rewarding neutrals while maintaining blockade against US/Israel/Western allies. IRGC toll booth system (yuan-denominated, $2M/transit, Larak Island escort) continues. 142 transits March 1-25 vs 2,652 baseline = 95% reduction PERSISTS despite selective opening. Trump's '20 boats' claim appears to conflate Pakistan deal with broader data. Trump's 'obliterate' post explicitly conditions infrastructure destruction on Hormuz not being 'immediately open for business.' April 6 deadline hardening.

EVENING UPDATE — PARADIGM SHIFT: WSJ reported Trump told aides he's willing to end military campaign even if Hormuz remains largely shut. Leavitt: Hormuz reopening 'not a core objective.' This BREAKS the market's assumed resolution path (war ends → Hormuz reopens → oil normalizes). Pakistan 20-ship deal EXECUTING — MV Multan (general cargo) and P-Akili (80M+ litres crude) crossed Hormuz, expected at Karachi March 31. Japan in negotiations — Araghchi says Iran 'prepared to allow.' Two Chinese-owned ultra-large containerships DENIED per Windward — individual vetting applies. Iran's parliament drafting toll legislation to formalize Hormuz authority — potentially making selective control PERMANENT regardless of ceasefire. IRGC toll continues: yuan-denominated, $2M/transit, Larak Island escort. Fewer than 10 commercial vessels/day vs normal 20-30.

WSJ paradigm shift CORROBORATED by CNN: 'Trump administration believes it will have to end Iran war with oil chokepoint still closed.' Leavitt called Hormuz reopening 'not a core objective.' Market pricing confirmation: Brent surged ~5% to ~$118.60 on April 1 DESPITE equities rallying on peace hopes — oil and equities moved in SAME direction. Oil up on structural supply concern, equities up on peace. The market is telling us: even if war ends, Hormuz disruption persists. Iran's parliament drafting toll legislation to formalize permanent selective control. IEA 400M barrel SPR release flowing at 1.0-1.5M bpd but US SPR drops to 243M barrels — lowest since 1984 — failing to contain prices. (Sources: WSJ, CNN, Bloomberg, DOE)

Al-Salmi VLCC attacked at Dubai port anchorage — 31 nautical miles NW of Dubai, OUTSIDE Hormuz proper. Demonstrates Iran willing to strike in allied Gulf waters, not just at chokepoint. War risk insurance must now reprice for entire Gulf, not just Hormuz transit. Concurrent: Greek container ship off Ras Tanura hit, Thai cargo ship near Hormuz (3 crew missing). Day 33 zero Houthi Red Sea shipping attacks — commercial closure via carrier withdrawal (Maersk, CMA CGM, Hapag-Lloyd, MSC rerouted), not military blockade. (Sources: Bloomberg, UKMTO, Maritime Executive, gCaptain)

CONFIRMED: Hormuz toll law passed FULL Majlis (not just committee). Codifies Tehran's 'sovereignty, control and oversight' over the Strait, establishes transit fees payable in Iranian rials, bans US/Israel-linked vessels and countries that joined unilateral sanctions. Still needs Guardian Council review + presidential signature. De facto ~$2M/vessel. GCC Secretary-General condemned — fees violate UNCLOS. US Naval War College: 'no legal basis.' GCC: 'will not accept bilateral arrangements.' This formalizes the WSJ/CNN Hormuz decoupling paradigm — Iran legislating permanent selective control regardless of ceasefire outcome. (Sources: Turkiye Today, Anadolu, CNN, Bloomberg)

CONFIRMED: AQUA 1 tanker struck 17nm north of Ras Laffan in Qatar waters — 3 cruise missiles fired, 2 intercepted, 1 hit. Demonstrates Iran's ability to project force across Gulf waters, not just in the Strait itself. Attack pattern expanding: Hormuz chokepoint → Dubai port (Al-Salmi VLCC) → Qatar waters (AQUA 1). Combined with toll law, Iran establishing de facto maritime control over entire Arabian Gulf. (Sources: QatarEnergy, Al Jazeera, AGBI)

CONFIRMED: War risk insurance at 1%+ hull value ($10-14M/voyage), 5x pre-crisis levels. Lloyd's JWC redesignated entire Arabian Gulf as conflict zone. Insurance premium IS the market's probability assessment — 'commercial risk logic has become an irregular warfare tool at Hormuz' per Irregular Warfare Journal. Hormuz tanker traffic collapsed 80%+. Commercial closure achieved through insurance costs making transit economically unviable, not through total military blockade. (Sources: Lloyd's List, S&P Global, Irregular Warfare Journal)

CONFIRMED: ~40 VLCCs anchored outside Yanbu with 5-day waits, 64 ships en route. Diverted Hormuz traffic is crushing Saudi Red Sea port capacity. Commercial closure is accomplished via carrier withdrawal (Maersk, CMA CGM, Hapag-Lloyd, MSC rerouted), not military blockade. Some transit continues through IRGC toll system but at 80%+ reduction from baseline. (Sources: maritime data, Bloomberg)

Iran's Majlis reportedly passed Hormuz toll law — codifying sovereignty, transit fees in rials, banning US/Israel/sanctions-allied vessels. Requires Guardian Council review + presidential signature (neither confirmed). GCC condemned as UNCLOS violation. This LEGISLATES the WSJ/CNN Hormuz decoupling paradigm — permanent selective control regardless of ceasefire. De facto tolls ~$2M/vessel via IRGC escort continue. 95% reduction persists. April 6 energy pause 3 days away. (Sources: Turkiye Today, Anadolu, CNN, Bloomberg)

April 6 deadline HOURS away. Trump 48-hour ultimatum: 'Open the f***in' Strait' or face destruction of power plants and bridges. Iran rejected: 'gates of hell.' Zero extension signaled. Zero diplomatic progress. Toll law still awaiting Guardian Council review. IRGC toll system continues (~$2M/transit, yuan-denominated). 95% reduction persists. If deadline expires without extension, US resumes energy infrastructure strikes. (Sources: Times of Israel, Al Jazeera, Fortune, Newsweek)

CONFIRMED: Deadline EXTENDED 20 hours to Tuesday April 7, 8 PM ET. Iran's IRGC responded with permanent closure threat: 'The Strait of Hormuz will be completely closed and will not be opened until our destroyed power plants are rebuilt.' This exceeds prior rhetoric — linking Hormuz reopening to RECONSTRUCTION, not just ceasefire. Separately, Oman-Iran Hormuz transit talks REPORTED (The National, Abu Dhabi) — Oman and Iran discussing 'measures for smooth transit,' potentially a parallel track for partial reopening short of full capitulation to Trump's demands. 45-day ceasefire framework (Axios) includes Hormuz as one of Iran's two primary bargaining chips (alongside HEU stockpile). Toll law still awaiting Guardian Council review. IRGC toll system continues. 95% reduction persists.

Bloomberg Hormuz Tracker: weekly transits hit highest since war began — 21 ships over April 5-6 weekend (13 outbound, 8 inbound). Iraqi crude tanker transited after Iran granted 'brotherly Iraq' exemption. Eight Indian LPG tankers now through. First Western-flagged vessel (French-owned) permitted April 3. Permitted nations: China, Russia, India, Iraq, Pakistan, Malaysia, Thailand, Philippines, select Western on case-by-case. HOWEVER 21/weekend is still ~1/7 of pre-war daily 138. IRGC 'New Persian Gulf Order' classifies countries as hostile/neutral/friendly — selective opening widening but tightly controlled. IRGC Navy: Hormuz will 'never return to its former state.' Iran's 10-clause ceasefire counter-proposal includes Hormuz protocol (not unconditional reopening). Tuesday deadline is TODAY. (Sources: Bloomberg Hormuz Tracker, Energy Connects, Seatrade Maritime, Al Jazeera, PressTV)

BLOOMBERG HORMUZ TRACKER: WEEKLY TRANSITS REACH WAR HIGH [CONFIRMED]. The seven-day rolling average for Hormuz transits has reached the highest level since the war began. Iran's three-tier system (hostile/neutral/friendly) is quietly working — vessels flagged to China, Russia, India, Iraq, Pakistan, Malaysia, Thailand, and the Philippines are transiting. Two Qatar LNG tankers were quietly allowed through under a Pakistan-brokered secret deal. Iraqi-linked oil tanker exited Hormuz via Iran on April 5. The 'closure' Trump is using as casus belli is now factually a partial selective opening — though crucially, it remains closed to Western/hostile-flagged vessels and OPEC+ exports cannot increase. Trump's 'Strait closed' pretext is partially fictional. Iran's strategy is winning quietly. Bloomberg's data is sourced from Vortexa and Kpler ship-tracking — independently verifiable.

IRAN'S 10-POINT COUNTER demands recognition of Iranian sovereignty over Hormuz + 'safe-passage protocol' (Iranian regulation, not free reopening). Trump's 'open the Strait or we destroy your power plants' demand is now partially met. Iran's position: 'It IS open — to friends. We will not open it to enemies.' The semantic gap is the war's fulcrum. If Trump accepts the partial reopening as 'compliance,' the deadline can lapse. If he insists on Western/Israeli access, escalation is locked in.

EVENING UPDATE: WTI-Brent inversion CONFIRMED — WTI $114.53 > Brent $110.80 ($4.60 premium). Market reclassifying Gulf crude as 'semi-stranded.' WTI Midland offered at $34 premium over Dubai benchmark (OilPrice.com). On like-for-like basis June Brent still above June WTI ($107.95 vs $96.36) — headline inversion partly contract-month mismatch, but underlying signal real. OPEC+ 206K bpd for May conditional on Hormuz reopening (Bloomberg) — paper decision, cannot be delivered. JPMorgan warned $150+ if Hormuz stays shut into mid-May. Iran's 10-point counter includes Hormuz 'safe passage protocol' — $2M transit fee split with Oman, revenue to reconstruction. Carnegie's Sadjadpour: Iran 'openly talking about formalizing Hormuz control as if it's going to be their own Panama Canal.' (Sources: CNBC, Bloomberg, OilPrice.com, JPMorgan, i24NEWS)

CEASEFIRE PROMISES 'COMPLETE OPENING' (TRUMP) vs 'CONTROLLED PASSAGE' (IRAN) — STRUCTURAL CONTRADICTION [CONFIRMED]. Trump's announcement of the 14-day ceasefire conditioned the suspension on Iran agreeing to a 'COMPLETE, IMMEDIATE, and SAFE OPENING' of the Strait of Hormuz. Iran's FM Araghchi specified the deal includes 'safe passage' through the Strait 'via coordination with Iran's Armed Forces' — the same selective-control framework Iran has maintained since the IRGC toll system was established March 27. This is NOT 'complete opening' in the Western sense — it is Iranian sovereignty over the Strait dressed as ceasefire compliance. The semantic gap is the war's structural fulcrum and the most likely point of failure when Friday April 10 Islamabad talks begin. Markets repriced as if 'complete opening' was real — Brent -13.5%. Lloyd's: physical normalisation cannot be compressed by political will; reinsurance restoration is sequential, multi-party, and takes weeks even on a holding ceasefire.

Iran RE-CLOSED the Strait of Hormuz on April 8-9 citing Israeli strikes on Lebanon as a ceasefire violation. Bloomberg ship-tracking data shows only 3-4 ships transited April 8-9, down from 11 on Tuesday and 9 on Monday. First post-ceasefire transits were 2 bulk carriers (Greek-owned NJ Earth and Liberia-flagged Daytona Beach) — NOT oil tankers. 479 vessels remain stranded including 426 tankers, 34 LPG carriers, 19 LNG ships per MarineTraffic. Pre-war throughput ~100-120 vessels/day; current ~4-5/day = 95%+ reduction persists. White House Press Secretary Leavitt called re-closure 'false'; Defense Secretary Hegseth: 'The strait is open' — both contradicted by Bloomberg operational data. CBS News timeline: even if ceasefire holds perfectly, oil exports return to previous levels by July 2026 at earliest.

War risk insurance rates dipped post-ceasefire to 0.35-0.45% of vessel value (from peak 3.5-10%), but remain 7-10x pre-war levels. For a $150M LNG carrier, still adds ~$500K-$675K per voyage. Neil Roberts (LMA): 'It is highly unlikely that trade into the Gulf will simply resume.' Reinsurers exercised 72-hour cancellation clauses at war start; restoring coverage takes weeks, not days. Lloyd's Market Association confirmed 88% of participants still have appetite to underwrite hull war risks.

Day 43. THREE CHINESE VLCCs (Cospearl Lake, He Rong Hai, Yuan Hua Hu) halted at strait, NOT through — ceasefire failed its first operational test. IRGC toll system remains in force. S&P Global: ~9 vessels transited April 8-10 combined vs 100+/day pre-war = ~3% capacity. Stranded vessel count revised UP: Bloomberg 800+, IMO 2,000, Press TV/Global Security 3,000 with 94% reduction. 20,000+ seafarers trapped. NBC News: 'effectively at a standstill despite ceasefire.' CNBC: 'may take months to recover.' War risk insurance at 1.5-3.0% hull value. LMA Neil Roberts: safety, not insurance, is the bottleneck. Russia-China vetoed Bahrain UNSC Hormuz resolution — closing multilateral reopening pathway. (Source: Bloomberg AIS, NBC News, CNBC, S&P Global, UN News)

edges.context: Day 41. Iran RE-CLOSED Hormuz citing Israeli Lebanon strikes as ceasefire violation. Toggle mechanism confirmed: Iran opens/closes keyed to Israeli compliance. Bloomberg AIS data: 3-4 ships April 8-9 vs 11 Tuesday. White House denial contradicted by operational data. 479 vessels stranded.

Day 41. Iran re-closed Hormuz within 18 hours of ceasefire — proving toggle mechanism. CBS News: oil exports return to previous levels by July 2026 at earliest even if ceasefire holds perfectly. War risk insurance 7-10x pre-war despite post-ceasefire dip. Physical flow restoration timeline measured in months, not days.

Major LNG exports from Qatar transit or are affected by Hormuz. Qatar halted Ras Laffan LNG production. AQUA 1 tanker struck near Ras Laffan. European TTF at EUR 47.51/MWh. EU storage at 30% (five-year seasonal low). Toll law + Gulf-wide attacks make LNG resumption impossible even with ceasefire.

Day 41. Re-closure: 3-4 ships April 8-9, 479 stranded (426 tankers, 34 LPG, 19 LNG). First post-ceasefire transits were bulk carriers NOT oil tankers. War risk insurance dipped to 0.35-0.45% but remains 7-10x pre-war. LMA: 'highly unlikely trade into the Gulf will simply resume.' Reinsurance restoration takes weeks.

50% of India's crude imports transit Hormuz. Also India's LPG imports (~91% from Gulf), fertilizer raw materials. Closure directly threatens India's energy and food security. Eight Indian LPG tankers now permitted through Hormuz.
Iraq's southern oil exports depend on Hormuz. Iraq declared force majeure on all foreign-operated oilfields (Mar 20), cut production from 3.3M to 900K bpd. 90%+ of Iraq's income from oil.

Saudi Yanbu bypass at 4.2M bpd is only functioning Gulf export route. But ~40 VLCCs anchored outside Yanbu with 5-day waits, 64 ships en route — capacity crushed by diverted Hormuz traffic. Saudi bypass being overwhelmed.

trigger_points.condition: Hormuz reopens to all traffic (even partially)
trigger_points.mechanism: Single biggest potential catalyst for oil price drop, India relief, rupee recovery, and equity market rebound. Would signal de-escalation. | UPDATE 2026-04-07: Bloomberg Hormuz Tracker confirms weekly transits at WAR HIGHS via Iran's three-tier system. THIS IS PARTIAL REOPENING in fact, though Iran calls it selective regulation rather than reopening. | UPDATE 2026-04-09: Iran RE-CLOSED Hormuz within 18 hours of ceasefire citing Israeli Lebanon strikes. Toggle mechanism confirmed — Iran can open/close at will keyed to Israeli compliance. Re-closure is NOT a new closure but a continuation of the controlled passage framework. Status remains WATCHING — the pattern is toggle (partial open/close), not sustained unconditional reopening.

trigger_points.condition: US attempts naval escort or forced opening of Hormuz
trigger_points.mechanism: Would risk direct naval confrontation with Iran. Could escalate to mining, submarine warfare. Oil could spike further. Major escalation risk.

trigger_points.condition: Hormuz remains under Iranian selective control even within ceasefire framework — ACTIVE 2026-04-08: Iran's 'controlled passage via coordination with armed forces' framing in the ceasefire text formalises the IRGC toll regime as the Iranian-side compliance mechanism. Even the bilateral US-Iran deal does NOT deliver unconditional Hormuz freedom. WSJ paradigm + Hormuz toll law + ceasefire 'controlled passage' language all converge.
trigger_points.mechanism: WSJ reports Trump willing to exit without Hormuz reopening. If war ends but Iran maintains toll system and selective passage, oil floor $90-100 permanently rather than reverting to $70. Changes the entire crisis-resolution investment thesis. | UPDATE 2026-04-07: Iran's 10-point counter formalizes 'safe-passage protocol' — i.e., permanent selective control under sovereignty claim. This is the structural Iranian position even pre-ceasefire. Trigger pre-conditions strengthening.

price_snapshots.trigger: Trump 'complete opening' vs Iran 'controlled passage' contradiction
price_snapshots.delta_context: Brent crashed -13.5% on the announcement; physical flows lag the political headline
